title: The Weak Case and Uncertain Benefits of Social Media Bans for Kids
content:
Around the world, governments are increasingly considering bans to prevent children from using social media. In Spain, a 15-year-old named Ramón admits that social media often negatively affects his attention and mental health. Despite these concerns, both Ramón and his mother believe that the Spanish government’s plan to ban children under 16 from these platforms will ultimately fail. They argue that a blanket ban sounds strong but actually pushes teenagers toward riskier, more secretive online behavior. Instead of an outright ban, they would prefer the government to try curbing the most harmful effects through careful regulation.

Spain is not alone, as countries like Australia, Britain, and France have also proposed or implemented similar age restrictions. The primary concern is that social apps provoke depression and expose children to online predators, leading to severe social harm. Books and studies have persuaded many politicians that smartphones are rewiring childhood in a malign way. Consequently, imposing social media bans commands widespread support across different demographic and political divides globally. However, translating this popular support into effective laws has proven to be incredibly difficult.

One major challenge is enforcing age verification without compromising user privacy or pushing kids toward unregulated platforms. Social media firms struggle to reliably verify ages, and tech-savvy teenagers often find ways to bypass these checks using artificial intelligence or virtual private networks. Furthermore, researchers point out that the amount of time spent on social media might not be as relevant as the actual content consumed. Many child-welfare groups even oppose blanket bans, arguing they create a false sense of security and leave older teens unprepared for the digital world. Ultimately, pressuring tech companies to design more child-friendly platforms may be a more effective solution than outright exclusion.
